key_insight: A fully differentiable JAX finite element framework can run anisotropic finite-strain plasticity simulations on GPUs while also providing accurate gradients for inverse material characterization.
engineering_relevance: This enables faster, scalable calibration of complex constitutive models from full-field data, reducing reliance on many costly physical experiments.
method: JAX-based GPU FEM with parallelized element evaluation, sparse assembly, and sparse linear solves
Automatic differentiation through constitutive updates and the solver for consistent Jacobians and PDE sensitivities
Inverse identification using topology-optimized heterogeneous specimens and displacement field measurements
evidence_signal: In a 3-million-DOF forward benchmark, the method reached up to 9.4× speed-up on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU versus a 24-core CPU Abaqus baseline, and it recovered anisotropic yield and hardening parameters in multiple inverse tests.
watch_item: Performance and gradient quality depend on careful GPU implementation and the conditioning of the inverse problem, especially for spatially varying parameters.
paper_title: A Differentiable GPU-Accelerated Finite Element Framework for Inverse Characterization of Finite-Strain Anisotropic Plasticity
